# depd

[![NPM Version][npm-image]][npm-url]
[![NPM Downloads][downloads-image]][downloads-url]
[![Node.js Version][node-version-image]][node-version-url]
[![Build Status][travis-image]][travis-url]
[![Test Coverage][coveralls-image]][coveralls-url]

Deprecation message utility for Node.js. This is used to declare that a function, method, property, or feature is deprecated and will be removed in a future version.

## Installation

```sh
$ npm install depd
```

## API

```js
var depd = require('depd')
```

### depd(namespace)

Create a new deprecate function that uses the given namespace name for the messages. This namespace will be prefixed to all deprecation messages emitted by the returned function.

```js
var deprecate = depd('my-cool-module')
```

The returned function is the `deprecate` function that can be called with a message to output a deprecation warning.

```js
deprecate('this method is deprecated')
```

When called, the deprecation message will be displayed to the user (depending on the process state) and the call site will be included in the message. The call site is obtained from the stack trace and attempts to be the first call site outside of the module.

#### Arguments

- `namespace` - The namespace for the deprecation warnings. This is typically the name of your module.

#### Return Value

The return value is a function that takes a message argument to emit a deprecation warning.

### depd.function(fn, message)

Wrap a function in a deprecation message. This returns a new function that emits a deprecation message when called and then calls the original function. The signature of the returned function matches the original function.

```js
var deprecate = depd('my-cool-module')

exports.oldMethod = deprecate.function(oldMethod, 'oldMethod() is deprecated, use newMethod() instead')

function oldMethod() {
  // Do something
}
```

#### Arguments

- `fn` - The function to wrap with a deprecation message.
- `message` - The message to output when the function is called.

#### Return Value

The return value is a function that emits a deprecation message when called and then calls the original function.

### depd.property(obj, prop, message)

Mark a property as deprecated. This wraps a property with a deprecation message and returns the wrapped property descriptor for use with `Object.defineProperty`. When accessed, the deprecation message will be emitted.

```js
var deprecate = depd('my-cool-module')

Object.defineProperty(exports, 'oldProperty', deprecate.property({
  get: function () { return 'the old value' }
}, 'oldProperty is deprecated, use newProperty instead'))
```

#### Arguments

- `obj` - The object with the property descriptor.
- `prop` - The property name.
- `message` - The message to output when the property is accessed.

#### Return Value

The return value is a property descriptor that will emit a deprecation message when accessed and then return the result of the original descriptor.

## Environment Variables

The following environment variables can be used to control the behavior of depd:

### NO_DEPRECATION

The `NO_DEPRECATION` environment variable can be set to suppress all deprecation warnings from being displayed. This is useful when you want to run tests without seeing deprecation warnings.

```sh
$ NO_DEPRECATION=* node app.js
```

You can also set this per-namespace:

```sh
$ NO_DEPRECATION=my-cool-module node app.js
```

### TRACE_DEPRECATION

The `TRACE_DEPRECATION` environment variable can be set to show a full stack trace with every deprecation warning.

```sh
$ TRACE_DEPRECATION=* node app.js
```

You can also set this per-namespace:

```sh
$ TRACE_DEPRECATION=my-cool-module node app.js
```

### THROW_DEPRECATION

The `THROW_DEPRECATION` environment variable can be set to make the deprecation warnings throw an exception. This is useful in tests to ensure no deprecated functions are being used.

```sh
$ THROW_DEPRECATION=* node app.js
```

You can also set this per-namespace:

```sh
$ THROW_DEPRECATION=my-cool-module node app.js
```

## Examples

### Deprecating a method

```js
var deprecate = require('depd')('my-cool-module')

// Deprecate oldMethod
exports.oldMethod = function () {
  deprecate('oldMethod() is deprecated, use newMethod() instead')
  // Continue with the method
}

// Deprecate oldMethod with a wrapped function
exports.oldMethod = deprecate.function(function () {
  // Continue with the method
}, 'oldMethod() is deprecated, use newMethod() instead')
```

### Deprecating a property

```js
var deprecate = require('depd')('my-cool-module')

exports.oldProperty = 'the old value'

// Deprecate getting oldProperty
Object.defineProperty(exports, 'oldProperty', {
  configurable: true,
  enumerable: true,
  get: function () {
    deprecate('oldProperty is deprecated, use newProperty instead')
    return 'the old value'
  }
})

// Deprecate both getting and setting with a property descriptor
Object.defineProperty(exports, 'oldProperty', deprecate.property({
  configurable: true,
  enumerable: true,
  get: function () { return 'the old value' },
  set: function (val) { /* set value */ }
}, 'oldProperty is deprecated, use newProperty instead'))
```

## Usage in Express.js

The `depd` package is used extensively in Express.js to mark deprecated methods and properties. When you see deprecation warnings in your Express.js application, they are generated using this module.

For example, in Express.js:

```js
var deprecate = require('depd')('express')

// Later in the code
if (something) {
  deprecate('something is deprecated, use somethingElse instead')
}
```

This helps Express.js maintain backward compatibility while signaling to developers which features will be removed in future versions.
